a: Some observed use cases are MRO inspection rather than new-part manufacturing.

q: Fixed-price defense work can convert gains into loss avoidance rather than incremental margin.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Aircraft Engine and Engine Parts Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Suppliers earn per-part or lot revenue under long-term, requirements-based engine-platform agreements, purchase orders, defense contracts, and qualified-source programs, with separate nonrecurring engineering, tooling, qualification, and sometimes aftermarket or repair revenue. Volumes are normally released rather than guaranteed; quality, traceability, delivery, cost-down, and airworthiness obligations are central.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to qualified independent component manufacturers with repeat engine-program production because the full code mixes a few dominant complete-engine OEMs with captive plants, independent parts makers, MRO operations, distributors, and development-stage propulsion companies whose economics and transferability are not comparable.
lens.excluded_activities: GE Aerospace, Pratt & Whitney, Rolls-Royce, Honeywell, and other complete-engine primes; captive divisions of global suppliers; pure MRO and repair shops without a manufacturing core; distributors and brokers; industrial gas-turbine-only businesses; experimental propulsion developers without repeat production; and general machine shops lacking aerospace qualification and recurring engine programs.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent manufacturers of certified aircraft-engine components and replacement parts under recurring OEM, Tier-1, defense, or FAA-PMA programs, including precision machining, forming, casting, additive production, coatings, welding, brazing, inspection, and assembly of hot-section, rotating, structural, fuel, and accessory components.

a.bridge: GE Aerospace's observed AI uses in turbine-blade image review, white-light inspection, nonconforming-hardware evaluation, parts prediction, engine monitoring, and design iteration are mapped from a global engine OEM and MRO network to the task mix of a smaller qualified engine-component manufacturer. The estimate includes inspection triage, quality engineering, process monitoring, maintenance, planning, quotation, traceability, documentation, design and manufacturing engineering support, while removing installed CNC, robotic, additive, and conventional inspection automation and retaining skilled machining, finishing, handling, destructive and non-destructive testing, and accountable disposition. GE's scale and data history likely bias the base upward; a high engineering and quality share can make the low case conservative.
caveats: GE's internal data, capital, and technical resources exceed those of typical LMM suppliers.
Some observed use cases are MRO inspection rather than new-part manufacturing.
The range measures technical task exposure, not removal of quality accountability.
a.change_evidence: A task-and-wage census from qualified LMM engine-component plants, segmented by process and current digital automation, plus audited labor outcomes from deployed inspection and engineering tools, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The sector has unusually concrete AI evidence: GE reports AI-guided turbine-blade review, robot inspection, an engineering and quality assistant trained on historical nonconformance data, materials prediction, and generative design iteration. Human technicians and engineers still capture images, validate findings, operate special processes, measure parts, and approve safety-critical dispositions.

d5.bridge: GE Aerospace's 2025 growth in engine deliveries, spare-parts volume, shop visits, and manufacturing and MRO capacity investment, combined with Boeing's long-term fleet and replacement outlook, is mapped to five-year real demand for qualified US LMM engine-component suppliers. The base reflects commercial production recovery, constrained supply, defense propulsion, installed-base utilization, and aftermarket replacement, while discounting large-OEM results for supplier-specific platform exposure and potential inventory normalization. Current capacity constraints may bias the base upward if they unwind; aging fleets and shop-visit demand may make it conservative.
caveats: Boeing's fleet outlook is a broad commercial-aircraft proxy rather than a direct engine-parts forecast.
OEM inventory correction or platform-specific technical problems can interrupt otherwise strong demand.
An LMM supplier's demand can be far more concentrated than aggregate industry growth.
d5.change_evidence: A part-number and engine-platform forecast for eligible suppliers covering OEM build rates, shop visits, retirement, life limits, spares, defense appropriations, inventories, and share-of-wallet would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: New-engine deliveries, spare-parts demand, shop visits, defense propulsion, and a growing and replacing aircraft fleet provide multiple demand channels. GE reported higher 2025 equipment and service revenue driven by engine deliveries, price, shop visits, and spare-parts volume while investing in production and MRO capacity. Individual suppliers remain exposed to engine-platform mix, destocking, qualification loss, and OEM production schedules.

caveats: Firm, establishment, and program-level classifications do not align cleanly.
Many suppliers combine engine components with aerostructure, defense, space, industrial, or repair revenue.
The frozen count is margin-bridged and may include businesses without transferable qualifications or durable programs.
e.change_evidence: A firm- and plant-level census linked to ownership, engine revenue, OEM versus aftermarket mix, certification, recurring-program status, process capability, EBITDA, and captive versus merchant production would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: A substantial independent supplier population performs tight-tolerance machining, brazing, fabrication, welding, composite, and other qualified aerospace production, and PMA creates a distinct independent replacement-parts pathway. Eligibility is reduced because a large share of industry receipts sits with a few complete-engine OEMs and captive plants, while the code also contains pure repair operations, distributors, industrial-turbine work, general shops, and development-stage businesses outside the recurring LMM lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336412 ($11,108,814,768) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336412 ($43,565,719,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $637,364 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336412); class <5: 68 firms, avg revenue/firm $818,426 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,986 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,213,633 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $200,112 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,066,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $277,205 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 91 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,606,473 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,230,025 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $43,790,158 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,958,630 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $986,721,512 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $89,199,625 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 129

caveats: This estimate excludes ordinary engine-build and shop-visit cycles represented in d5.
Part consolidation can eliminate a supplier's entire position even if aggregate engine demand grows.
Electric or hybrid propulsion adoption is likely to affect light aircraft sooner than large commercial fleets.
o.change_evidence: Engine-OEM sourcing roadmaps quantifying insourcing, additive part consolidation, durability changes, alternative propulsion, supplier rationalization, and platform retirement by affected part number would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Aircraft propulsion continues to require physical, certified components and replacement parts; software cannot eliminate that need. Five-year outside displacement comes from OEM insourcing, additive-manufacturing consolidation of multiple parts, alternative propulsion architectures, improved durability reducing replacement frequency, platform retirement, and foreign sourcing. These can materially affect a concentrated supplier but are unlikely to displace much aggregate eligible demand within five years.

q.bridge: Park Aerospace's requirements-based long-term pricing agreements, concentrated customer base, qualification burden, and non-guaranteed quantities, together with GE Aerospace's observed ability to combine cost productivity with product and service pricing, are mapped to five-year retention of AI-created operating surplus at an eligible engine-parts supplier. Customer concentration and OEM sourcing power reduce retention relative to GE; lengthy qualification, scarce capacity, proprietary processes, and high switching costs support more retention than automotive commodity parts. Park's composites business is adjacent rather than engine-specific and may bias the bridge in either direction.
caveats: Park Aerospace is an adjacent qualified aerospace materials and structures supplier, not a pure engine-parts manufacturer.
Retention differs sharply between proprietary PMA parts and build-to-print OEM components.
Fixed-price defense work can convert gains into loss avoidance rather than incremental margin.
q.change_evidence: Actual engine-component LTAs showing price-escalation and cost-down clauses, open-book requirements, productivity sharing, rebid timing, qualification switching cost, and realized margins after automation would materially change this value.
q.rationale: Long-term agreements and customer forecasts create recurring positions but do not guarantee volume, and major engine OEMs can demand productivity, dual-source parts, or reprice at renewal. Suppliers retain more of hidden yield, scrap, inspection, and schedule gains between negotiations, especially on difficult qualified parts with constrained capacity. The five-year base assumes meaningful but incomplete sharing.

rho.bridge: GE's deployed AI inspection and engineering tools are translated into five-year realization at LMM suppliers and discounted for FAA-PMA and OEM qualification requirements, customer approvals, validated special processes, data integrity, cybersecurity, model monitoring, legacy equipment, and scarce technical staff. PMA evidence shows that both design and the production quality system must be approved, while Park Aerospace shows lengthy customer qualification with no assured sales. Proven technician-in-the-loop tools support the base, but GE's scale and installed digital infrastructure may overstate smaller-plant realization.
caveats: Safety-critical disposition remains human-accountable even when AI prioritizes evidence.
Many LMM plants have fragmented inspection, ERP, and machine data.
Customer-specific approvals may prevent rapid reuse of a tool across engine programs.
rho.change_evidence: Comparable supplier deployments showing qualification path, validation time, false-call rates, integration cost, labor hours, yield, scrap, and performance across multiple part numbers and customers would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Assistive inspection, maintenance, scheduling, document, and knowledge tools can be introduced while preserving human sign-off. Tools that change design, process parameters, nonconformance disposition, or release decisions face much higher validation and customer-approval burdens. Realized value will often be lower scrap, fewer escapes, better yield, and shorter lead time rather than direct payroll reduction.

s5.bridge: Barnes's completed acquisition of precision aero-engine component manufacturer MB Aerospace and Warburg Pincus and Berkshire Partners' completed take-private of aerospace systems and proprietary-components supplier Triumph are observed transfers in the broader qualified aerospace-component ecosystem, mapped to the probability that an eligible US LMM engine-parts supplier changes control within five years. Both precedents exceed the target scale and Triumph spans systems, components, OEM, and aftermarket work, so the base is discounted for scale and product mismatch. Active sponsor and strategic consolidation may overstate availability of founder-held small firms, while private add-ons are under-disclosed.
caveats: Both disclosed precedents are materially larger than the target lens.
Buyer interest may concentrate in suppliers with proprietary IP or scarce engine-platform positions.
Customer qualification and program assignment can complicate an otherwise willing sale.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year denominator of independent US qualified engine-component firms matched to control sales, add-ons, recapitalizations, closures, and failed processes, with scale and product detail, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Qualified engine-component capabilities, proprietary processes, long program positions, and scarce capacity attract strategic and financial buyers. Availability is meaningful but constrained by founder ownership, customer concentration, assignment and change-of-control provisions, qualification transfer, capital intensity, environmental liabilities, export controls, and customer concern over continuity.

principal_driver: Demonstrated technician-in-the-loop AI for engine inspection and quality combines with strong production, spares, and shop-visit demand in a qualification-protected supply chain.
principal_weakness: A few engine OEMs control sourcing and qualification, so customer concentration, uncommitted LTA volumes, and program-specific approvals can overwhelm otherwise attractive AI productivity.
